title: Compress the Halden storyline to an IC one-pager
guidance: Reward the version that leads with the answer and would help a partner reach a call; tone should read partner-grade. Penalise even coverage with no verdict.

parts.label: Headline
parts.note: one bold line, states the call

runner_input.instruction: Compress the material below into an IC one-pager: lead with the answer, then the few things that change the decision.
runner_input.constraints: Output: markdown — one bold title line, then exactly three bullets.

source_blocks.text: Halden owns the Halden Code, the de-facto UK standard for food-safety management schedules, used by food manufacturers, hospitality groups and contract caterers. PE buyout under review by Aldgate Capital. All figures synthetic.
Overall: AI is a net positive; the moat is institutional, no funded challenger, three AI levers. Buy, gated on integration-contract terms and ML capability.

items: ARR £11.2m; recurring 94%; gross margin 88%; Adj. EBITDA £3.1m (30%); FCF £2.4m
HaldenOS platform on 48% of ~900 clients, up from 22% a year ago; 18% ARR uplift on migrations
NRR 112%; GRR 91%; NPS +58; voluntary attrition 6.1%; blended ACV ~£12k
FY31F plan: recurring billings £39.5m (~3.4x; 28% CAGR); Adj. EBITDA £14.2m (36%); price assumption 4% vs 5.8% realised
1,180 ratified schedules across 90 process types; IFSM endorsement with a 12-year exclusivity
Named in BRCGS audit guidance and contract-catering tenders; embedded in 14 QMS-software integrations
Buyers: food manufacturers 38% of ARR, hospitality & contract caterers 34%, retail 16%, other 12%
AutoSchedule (AI process-to-schedule mapping) shipped Jan 2026; £140k ARR by month 4; 88–95% accuracy on a 12,000-SKU test
Proprietary AI authoring tool used by the 8-FTE food-safety author team; an analytics module was pulled forward

source_blocks.text: The one credible AI-enabled disruption path is a QMS/food-safety software vendor using LLMs to generate a competing schedule library, routing around the integration dependency, and bidding for trade-body and auditor recognition — five sequential steps over 5–8 years. Customer DIY fails at the auditor gate (needs a citable, ratified source) and the allergen-liability gate (Natasha's Law is criminally enforced).

The CIM org chart lists an 18-FTE engineering team: 11 developers, 1 DevOps, 2 product, 3 QA — and no ML or data engineer. AutoSchedule's accuracy implies real ML plumbing; the heavy model calls run through a vendor API. The CTO is praised across interviews.

rubric_notes: Verdict: buy, gated on integration-contract terms and ML capability.
Foreground the capability gap as the upside-sizing risk.
